Per-Primitive Findings

Figure 1. Accuracy across all evaluated tasks, grouped by perceptual primitive. Dashed lines at 50% and 90%.

1. Counting / Enumeration

  • Solved Charts, tables, diagrams, and pie slices are perfect (99%, n=1,636) regardless of element count, because each element is visually distinct and structurally organized.
  • Overlap is the primary failure axis for shape counting. Pentagons at overlap 0.3 collapse to 0% for counts 4–6; circles fare better (90% at counts 5–9) but still overcount at high overlap. Shape complexity compounds: pentagons (60%) are 30pp harder than circles (90%) at matched count ranges.
  • Text labels rescue grid counting entirely. Blank grids drop from 100% (with text) to 50% (without), hitting 0% at 9+ rows. The model reads labels, not grid lines.
  • Olympic-rings memorization bias accounts for 73% of the HF circle gap. The model predicts "5" for 32% of 6-circle and 31% of 7-circle HF images due to the 3-over-2 colored-ring layout, but never in monochrome generated images.
Representative counting failures: pentagon overcounting at overlap, grid row off-by-one, circle overcounting with dense overlap.

2. Spatial Localization

  • Solved Table cell lookup (100%, 480 instances), diagram navigation (100%), simple bar/pie value reading with labels (100%) — structured, low-clutter localization is not a blind spot even at 10×5 grids.
  • Series confusion drives chart value degradation. Grouped bar and line chart value reading drop from ~100% at 2–3 series to ~50% at 9–10 series. The model finds the right x-position but attributes the value to the wrong series.
  • Pie value estimation without labels is near-chance (53%). With percentage labels: 100%. Without: the model must estimate angular proportions, achieving only mean absolute error of 2.8pp.
  • Circled-letter errors are off-by-one 70% of the time. At 82.4% overall, failures cluster on narrow characters (lowercase ‘l’: 8% accuracy) and positions where language priors override vision (position 14 of “Acknowledgement” → model hallucinates “s”).
Series confusion in dense line chart, arrow tracing failure in multi-decision DAG, and lowercase letter invisible next to uppercase.

3. Line / Path Following

  • Solved Diagram decision following (100%, 450 instances) and trend detection with named series at 2–4 lines (100%) — discrete labeled navigation and low-clutter trend reading are ceiling tasks.
  • Path following collapses with distractor routes. Subway-map path counting drops from 100% at 1–3 paths to 10% at 6 total connections with 1 target. 97% of errors are overcounts — the model counts all visible paths instead of filtering by endpoint.
  • Intersection counting hallucinates crossings at gt=0. At 5-point polylines the model always reports intersections even when there are none (0% accuracy at gt=0). It interprets visual proximity as crossing.
  • Trend detection degrades to 81% at 10 overlapping lines. The model must isolate a single named series through dense clutter — the same path-tracing skill that fails in subway maps.
Path overcounting with distractors, hallucinated intersection from proximity, and trend misread in 10-line chart.

4. Relative Comparison

  • Solved Table max (100%, 240 instances), bar comparison at diff≥2 (100%, 500 instances), and clearly separated circles (100%) — discrete labeled comparison is perfect regardless of distractor count.
  • Bar height comparison has a razor-sharp cliff at ~1% relative difference. At diff=1 (1–1.7% relative) accuracy drops to 91%; at diff=2 (~2–3%) it is already 100%. No gradual degradation — the threshold is binary.
  • Proximity detection has a hard ~12–15px floor. Below ~12px visible gap between circle edges, the model defaults to “touching” (2–8% accuracy at 1–4px gaps). Errors are exclusively false positives — a systematic bias toward reporting contact.
  • Angular comparison (pie) is far harder than height comparison (bars). Pie largest-slice identification is 73% vs. bar comparison at 100% for equivalent proportional differences. Pie imprecision also degrades cross-chart data matching (bar-vs-pie same-data detection: 75%).
Bar diff=1 misread as equal, and line comparison failures with compressed y-axis scale.

5. Color Discrimination

  • Solved Distinct hue families and color grids with ΔL≥16 are perfect (100%, 230 instances). The model’s color vocabulary handles typical chart palettes with well-separated colors.
  • The perceptual cliff falls at ΔL≈7–10. Color grid odd-cell detection: 86% at ΔL≈10, 60% at ΔL≈7, 21% at ΔL≈5 (near random). The threshold is sharp, not gradual.
  • Green is uniquely fragile; orange is uniquely robust. At extreme difficulty (ΔL≈5), orange maintains 55% while greens collapse to 15% and blues to 5%. This asymmetry persists across all tasks.
  • Heatmap value reading is the hardest color task (56%). It compounds three sub-skills: cell localization, color reading, and colorbar interpolation. The model reads the approximate color region but cannot interpolate the scale precisely (87% within ±10, but only 26% exact).
Blue grid at ΔL≈5 (shade invisible), green grid at ΔL≈7 (hardest family), and same-family bar legend confusion.

6. Text Reading (OCR)

  • Solved Words and numbers at font≥14px are near-perfect (99%, 300 instances) across all rotations and contrast levels. Typical chart labels fall in this safe zone.
  • The 10px→8px cliff drops words by 38pp (83%→45%) and numbers by 35pp (55%→20%). A 2px reduction causes the steepest accuracy drop in the entire eval. At 6px, the model is near-zero.
  • Rotation compounds multiplicatively with small font. Font=8 at 0° is 100%; at 45° it collapses to 7% for words and 0% for numbers. The interaction is severe — small rotated text is essentially invisible.
  • Numbers degrade faster than words because they lack lexical rescue. At font=8 the model hallucinates plausible words (“Compliance”→“Suitcase”) but numbers show raw digit-level failures (“365”→“55”) with no prior to fill in gaps.
Lexical hallucination at 6px/90°, digit-drop at 10px/90°, and the 8px cliff at 30° rotation.

7. Prior / Bias Override

  • No ceiling tasks Every task shows significant bias-induced errors — this is the only primitive with zero ceiling tasks.
  • Logo counting is complete vision bypass: 1.9% accuracy, 99.8% bias-aligned errors. The model retrieves canonical element counts from memory (Nike swoosh = 1, BMW = 4 rings) and never engages visual counting. Car logos are 0%; shoe logos 5.6%.
  • Patterned grid add-anomalies are invisible (0%). When an extra shape is added to a grid cell, the model reads it as matching the surrounding pattern. It reports 3–4 shapes (the canonical count) instead of the actual 1. Extended thinking (+1.6pp) does not help — the failure is perceptual, not reasoning.
  • Flag counting is 40% with 83% of errors being the exact biased answer. Star and stripe counts default to canonical values. The strength of the prior scales with familiarity: logos (99.8% bias) > flags (83%) > patterned grids.
Car logo retrieves canonical count, flag defaults to canonical star count, patterned grid add-anomaly invisible.

8. Visual-Textual Consistency

  • Solved Title trend conflicts (100%) and legend color conflicts (100%) — the model reads visual data and ignores contradictory titles and swapped legend labels.
  • Value labels are trusted absolutely: 0% accuracy when labels are wrong. When a bar displays a wrong number above it, the model reads the text label every time and never estimates the actual bar height. This is the single clearest grounding failure.
  • Annotations fool the model when visual evidence is ambiguous. A misleading “Highest” arrow causes 25% errors overall, but errors concentrate where the true max and annotated bar differ by <15 units (mean gap 11 for errors vs. 30 for correct responses).
  • The vulnerability is at the character level, not the semantic level. The model reasons past wrong titles (semantic) but cannot reason past wrong numbers placed directly on bars (character-level). It fails exactly where it looks for the answer.
Value label always trusted over bar height, annotation fools model at small gap, title correctly ignored.
